meta.title: W247 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W247 engine — M282, M260, OM654, OM608. Real UK costs, reliability data, and the OM654 diesel that sets the standard. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes W247 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W247 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only, depending on the engine — the OM654 diesel sits at £2,800–£5,000, while the M282 petrol is cheaper at £1,800–£3,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which W247 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 2.0-litre diesel (B200d/B220d) is the most reliable choice — it's Mercedes-developed, refined, and has a strong track record when serviced properly. The M260 2.0-litre petrol is also very dependable. Avoid the early OM608 1.5-litre diesel, which was phased out for good reason.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the W247?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early OM654 diesel engines (2018–2020) can suffer from cylinder head bolt failure, leading to coolant and oil leaks. The M282 petrol engine (Renault-sourced) may experience timing chain wear, and the M260 petrol can develop water pump and coolant leaks. The PHEV's battery and cooling system are also worth monitoring.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes W247 reliable overall?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the W247 is generally well-regarded for reliability, especially with the OM654 diesel and M260 petrol. The Renault-sourced M282 and OM608 are less refined and have more reported issues, but they are still acceptable for lower-mileage use. Regular servicing is key to longevity.

mainEntity.name: Which W247 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM608 1.5-litre diesel (early B180d) — it's a Renault unit that suffered from injector and DPF problems and was quickly replaced by the OM654. Also, any early OM654 without documented head bolt inspection should be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W247 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Warning signs include persistent coolant loss (OM654), cold-start rattle (M282), overheating (M260), or a sudden drop in electric range (PHEV). If you notice any of these, have the car inspected immediately — ignoring them can lead to a full engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes W247 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. A well-kept W247 is worth £12,000–£25,000, so a £2,800–£5,000 OM654 recon is only 11–42% of its value — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. On a high-mileage, lower-value example, the economics may shift — we always advise running the numbers.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W247 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can easily exceed 150,000 miles with regular servicing and early attention to the head bolt issue. The M260 petrol is similarly durable. The M282 and OM608 are less robust but can still reach 120,000 miles with meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W247 and the previous W246?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W247 is the third-generation B-Class, built on the newer MFA2 platform (shared with the A-Class and CLA). It introduced a completely new engine lineup, improved interior space, and updated technology. The W246 (2011–2018) used older OM651 and M270 engines and is covered on its own generation page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W247 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen), on the vehicle documentation, and stamped on the engine block. For the W247, common codes are M282 (petrol), M260 (petrol), OM654 (diesel), and OM608 (early diesel). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes B-Class W247 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the third-generation B-Class Sports Tourer — from the 1.3-litre turbo petrol to the 2.0-litre diesel and the plug-in hybrid. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM654 diesel that redefined the compact MPV. Backed by 520+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes B-Class W247 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W247 specifically — including the Renault-sourced M282 petrol, the Mercedes-developed M260, and the OM654 diesel family.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * OM654.920: 95% confidence — inherited from the Model Page's verified figures for the wider Mercedes range. * M260.920: 90% confidence — well-documented Mercedes engine, pattern data. * M282.914: 80% confidence — Renault-sourced, pattern data; provisional rating. * OM608.915: 70% confidence — phased out, limited data; provisional. * M282 PHEV: 70% confidence — emerging technology, provisional.

overview.intro: The W247 is the third generation of the Mercedes-Benz B-Class, launched in December 2018 as the successor to the W246. Built on the Mercedes MFA2 platform shared with the A-Class (W177) and CLA (C118), it introduced a new range of engines including the Renault-sourced M282 1.3-litre turbo petrol, the Mercedes-developed M260 2.0-litre turbo petrol, and the OM654 2.0-litre diesel family. A facelift arrived in October 2022 with mild-hybrid assistance across most variants.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MFA2 (shared with A-Class W177, CLA C118, GLA H247) • Predecessor: W246 (2011–2018) • Successor: currently in production (no successor announced) • Sibling body styles in this generation: none (B-Class is standalone Sports Tourer)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W247 B-Class generated 520+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM654 diesel as the most-requested powertrain.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.0-litre diesel, introduced in 2016, replaces the ageing OM651 with a lighter, more efficient design. It's the pick of the W247 range — smooth, torquey, and backed by a strong reliability record.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel without the Renault-sourced compromises.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.0-litre turbo petrol, delivering 190–224 bhp with a responsive character and strong torque. It's the enthusiast's choice in the W247 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want genuine performance from a compact MPV.

items.quote: The Renault-sourced 1.3-litre turbo petrol is a co-development engine (also used in Renault, Nissan, and Dacia models). While generally reliable, it lacks the refinement and long-term durability of Mercedes' own engines.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a budget who prioritise low running costs over longevity.

items.quote: A known issue on early OM654 engines where cylinder head bolts can break, leading to coolant and oil leaks. Repair costs can be significant if caught late.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who can afford the premium for the top diesel.

items.quote: The mid-range diesel offers the best balance of performance (150 bhp), economy (up to 67 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the W247 range.

The plug-in hybrid combines the 1.3-litre petrol with an electric motor for 218 bhp and impressive economy (up to 235 mpg). It's often overlooked in favour of the diesels but offers the lowest running costs for urban drivers.
items.whoItsFor: city drivers with access to charging.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Good Value — cheap to run, but watch for chain wear.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Cylinder head bolt issue (early), EGR cooler
Cylinder head bolt issue (early), turbo wear
rows.ownershipVerdict: Most Powerful — premium diesel, premium costs.
Avoid — Renault-sourced, phased out for good reason.
Urban Hero — low running costs if charged regularly.

economicsRule.title: The W247 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £12,000–£25,000 W247 sits at 11–42% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes threshold. On a higher-mileage B180d with the OM608, the same maths looks less favourable — we'll honestly tell you to consider the OM654 variant instead. Value the car first, then the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W247 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: B200d (OM654) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W247 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: B250e (PHEV) — battery and cooling system costs add up.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W247 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: B200d (OM654) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: OM654 Cylinder Head Bolt Failure (early units)
problems.rootCause: A manufacturing issue on early OM654 engines where cylinder head bolts can break, leading to coolant and oil leaks. Mercedes issued a technical service bulletin and revised the bolt specification.
problems.recommendation: If your W247 diesel was built before mid-2020, have the cylinder head bolts inspected at the next service — a £600–£1,200 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 replacement that follows a full failure.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your W247 diesel →

problems.title: M282 Timing Chain Wear (Renault-sourced petrol)
problems.rootCause: The M282 is a co-development engine with Renault/Nissan. While generally reliable, the timing chain tensioner can wear, leading to rattling on cold start and, if ignored, catastrophic failure.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the timing chain inspected immediately — a £500–£1,200 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,800–£3,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M282 engine prices for your W247 petrol →

problems.title: M260 Water Pump & Coolant Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M260's water pump and plastic coolant fittings can degrade over time, leading to coolant loss and potential overheating.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 60,000 miles — a £400–£800 repair is cheap insurance against a £2,500–£4,000 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M260 engine prices for your W247 4MATIC →
